patterns.title: Definite articles — el, la, los, las
patterns.explanation: Spanish nouns have gender. Masculine singular: el. Feminine singular: la. Masculine plural: los. Feminine plural: las.
patterns.hint: El = masculine singular, la = feminine singular.

patterns.title: Indefinite articles — un, una, unos, unas
patterns.explanation: Indefinite articles: un (masc. sing.), una (fem. sing.), unos (masc. pl.), unas (fem. pl.).

patterns.title: Present tense — ser (to be, permanent)
patterns.explanation: ser: yo soy, tú eres, él/ella es, nosotros somos, vosotros sois, ellos son. Used for identity, origin, profession, characteristics.
patterns.hint: Conjugate ser to match the subject.

patterns.title: Present tense — estar (to be, temporary)
patterns.explanation: estar: yo estoy, tú estás, él está, nosotros estamos, vosotros estáis, ellos están. Used for states, locations, feelings.
patterns.hint: Conjugate estar to match the subject.

patterns.title: Ser vs estar
patterns.explanation: ser = permanent/identity (nationality, profession, characteristics). estar = temporary state, location, feelings.

sentences.text: El libro está en la mesa.
El libro es en la mesa.

patterns.title: Present tense — regular -ar verbs
patterns.explanation: Regular -ar verbs: hablar → hablo, hablas, habla, hablamos, habláis, hablan. Remove -ar, add endings.
patterns.hint: Yo form of -ar verbs ends in -o.

patterns.title: Present tense — regular -er and -ir verbs
patterns.explanation: -er verbs: como, comes, come, comemos, coméis, comen. -ir verbs: vivo, vives, vive, vivimos, vivís, viven.
patterns.hint: Ella form of -er/-ir verbs ends in -e.

patterns.title: Basic sentence order — SVO
patterns.explanation: Spanish is generally Subject-Verb-Object, but word order is flexible. The subject pronoun is often dropped because the verb ending shows who is acting.

answers.note: Time adverb can come before or after the object.
Subject pronoun can be dropped — verb ending shows subject.

patterns.title: Preterite — regular -ar verbs
patterns.explanation: Preterite (completed past): hablar → hablé, hablaste, habló, hablamos, hablasteis, hablaron. Note accent on yo and él/ella forms.
patterns.template: Ayer yo ___ {[con mi amigo:hablé|mucho:estudié|en el restaurante:comí|la película:vi]}.
patterns.hint: Yo preterite of -ar verbs ends in -é (with accent).

patterns.title: Preterite vs imperfect — ser/estar
patterns.explanation: Preterite: specific completed event. Imperfect: habitual past, background description, ongoing state. fui/estuve = specific occasion. era/estaba = used to be / was (ongoing).

sentences.text: Cuando era niño, vivía en Madrid.
Cuando era niño, viví en Madrid.
sentences.error: Habitual/ongoing past states use imperfect (vivía), not preterite.
Specific completed events use preterite (fui), not imperfect.

patterns.title: Negation — no before verb
patterns.explanation: In Spanish, no comes immediately before the verb. Double negatives are required: no ... nada, no ... nadie, no ... nunca.

sentences.error: With negative words like nada, no must still precede the verb: no como nada.

patterns.title: Reflexive verbs — me, te, se, nos, os, se
patterns.explanation: Reflexive verbs have a pronoun that refers back to the subject. llamarse (to be called): me llamo, te llamas, se llama...
patterns.template: Yo ___ {[llamo Juan:me llamo|levanto a las siete:me levanto|lavo las manos:me lavo|ducho por la mañana:me ducho]}.
patterns.hint: Reflexive pronoun me agrees with yo.

patterns.title: Adjective agreement — gender and number
patterns.explanation: Adjectives must agree with the noun in gender and number. bueno → buena (fem.), buenos (masc. pl.), buenas (fem. pl.).
patterns.hint: Adjectives agree with the noun they describe. película is feminine.

patterns.title: Present subjunctive — with querer que
patterns.explanation: The subjunctive is used after expressions of wish, emotion, doubt, and impersonal expressions. quiero que + subjunctive (different subject).
patterns.hint: After querer que with a different subject, use the subjunctive.

patterns.title: Subjunctive vs indicative after cuando
patterns.explanation: cuando + indicative = when (habitual/past). cuando + subjunctive = when (future). E.g. Cuando llegues (future), llámame.

sentences.error: Future 'when' clauses require subjunctive: cuando llegues.

sentences.text: Cuando llego a casa, siempre como.
Cuando llegue a casa ayer, comí.

patterns.title: Ir a + infinitive — near future
patterns.explanation: ir a + infinitive expresses plans or intentions in the near future, similar to 'going to' in English.
patterns.template: Esta tarde voy a ___ {[estudiar|comer|ver una película|salir con amigos]}.
patterns.hint: voy a + infinitive = I'm going to [do something].

patterns.title: Object pronouns — position before verb
patterns.explanation: Direct and indirect object pronouns come before conjugated verbs: lo veo, le doy. They attach to infinitives and gerunds: verlo, dándole.

patterns.title: Conditional tense — would
patterns.explanation: Conditional: infinitive + -ía, -ías, -ía, -íamos, -íais, -ían. Used for hypothetical situations and polite requests.
patterns.template: Si tuviera tiempo, ___ {[estudiaría más|viajaría por el mundo|leería más libros|aprendería japonés]}.

patterns.title: Imperfect subjunctive — si clauses
patterns.explanation: Contrary-to-fact conditionals: Si + imperfect subjunctive + conditional. Si tuviera → if I had (but I don't).

sentences.error: Never use conditional in the si clause. Use imperfect subjunctive (tuviera) or present indicative (tengo).
The result clause needs conditional (viajaría), not imperfect.

patterns.title: Por vs para
patterns.explanation: para = purpose/goal/recipient/deadline. por = cause/reason/exchange/duration/on behalf of. Often confused.
patterns.hint: para = for (recipient, purpose). por = because of, in exchange for, during.

patterns.title: Inversion in questions
patterns.explanation: In Spanish questions, the subject follows the verb (inversion). ¿Habla Juan español? Also used after certain adverbs at the start.

answers.note: Object can also precede the subject in questions.

patterns.title: Present perfect — haber + participio
patterns.explanation: The present perfect (pretérito perfecto) is formed with haber + past participle. Used for recent past or actions connected to the present. Haber: he, has, ha, hemos, habéis, han.
patterns.template: Hoy yo ___ {[he comido|he estudiado|he viajado|he hablado]} mucho.
